sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives, the Northern Territory and Western Australian agriculture departments, Business Queensland, AgriFutures, and university and horticultural research.

core.intro: Rambutan is a true equatorial tree, so wherever you grow it in Australia the essentials are the same: a hot, humid, frost-free spot, deep free-draining acid soil, plenty of water, a named grafted or marcotted tree, and ideally a second tree to pollinate it. It is the hairy cousin of the lychee and longan and grows much the same way, but it is fussier about warmth and water and it usually needs a pollen partner to fruit well. The state sections cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere warm enough to grow the tree.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Rambutan does not come true from seed, so the quality fruit is grown from named clones. The benchmarks sold in Australia are the Malaysian R series (R134, R162 and R167 are the ones most often planted, with R3, R7 and R9 among the others), the Thai favourites Rongrien and Si Chompoo, the Indonesian Binjai, and Jitlee from Singapore. Skin colour runs from deep red to yellow depending on the clone, and red-skinned types are generally less troubled by insects than yellow ones. The eating quality to look for is thick, sweet, crisp flesh that peels cleanly off the seed (a freestone, testa-free aril) rather than flesh that clings. In practice many Australian nurseries sell rambutan as a marcotted or grafted tree labelled simply red or yellow rather than by clone, so ask which clone or seedling line it is and whether you will need a second tree to pollinate it. Rambutan shares its needs and many of its habits with its close relatives the lychee and longan.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: This is the one place rambutan differs sharply from its cousins. Most named clones carry almost only female-functioning flowers and make very little pollen, often well under one percent of the flowers on a panicle, so a lone tree of a single clone tends to set a light crop of small, seedless, pulpless fruit. To crop well the tree needs a pollen source nearby, which is why orchardists plant more than one cultivar in mixed rows, keep a pollen-rich tree or a seedling in the block, and rely on bees to carry the pollen between trees. Some growers go further and spray a plant hormone (NAA) to push a shy clone into making more male flowers. The practical upshot for a backyard is simple: where a longan will fruit on its own, a rambutan does much better with a second, different tree planted close by and plenty of bee activity at flowering.

sections.heading: Planting, soil and shelter
sections.body: Plant in a warm, frost-free, sheltered position in deep, well-drained soil that is rich in organic matter and acidic (about pH 4.5 to 6.5, ideally 5.5 to 6.5); rambutan does poorly on alkaline, chalky or waterlogged ground. Young trees are tender and sunburn easily, so shade them for their first two or three years and only then let them into full sun. Shelter from wind matters as much as anything, because the wood is brittle and even a moderate breeze slows growth, so plant behind a windbreak. Mulch heavily to keep the shallow roots cool and damp. Warm soil and the start of the wet season get a new tree away fastest, so spring into early summer is the usual planting time (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Rambutan is an equatorial tree that cannot handle water stress: short it of water in warm, dry, sunny weather and it sheds its half-grown fruit, because a single panicle may carry around a dozen fruit that each need a lot of water to swell. Keep the soil evenly moist all year under a thick mulch, and water generously while the fruit is filling (a mature tree may need a couple of thousand litres a week in a hot dry spell, and daily watering on sandy ground). On feeding, lean on potassium and go easy on nitrogen, since too much nitrogen pushes leaves at the expense of flowers and can worsen fruit drop as the seed hardens. There is one rule particular to rambutan: never feed it muriate of potash or any other chloride fertiliser, because the tree is very sensitive to chloride and responds with scorched leaf margins and leaf loss. Use potassium nitrate or sulphate of potash instead, and let a leaf or soil test guide the rest.

sections.heading: Harvest and eating
sections.body: Rambutan does not ripen after picking (it is non-climacteric), so pick the fruit only once it is fully coloured, which is about four to five months after flowering. The hairy skin and its soft spines look their best from roughly two to four weeks after the colour first breaks, and that is the window to pick. Always taste a sample first, because red clones do not reach full sweetness as early as their colour suggests; ripe fruit runs to about 18 to 21 percent sugar. The fruit has a very short life once picked, declining within a few days at room temperature as the hairs dry out and darken before the flesh does, so eat it fresh or refrigerate it promptly (it keeps a week or two held cool and humid, though it is chilling-sensitive and should not be stored too cold). A grafted or marcotted named tree usually starts bearing about three to five years after planting.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted or marcotted (air-layered) tree, never an unnamed seedling. Rambutan does not come true from seed, a seedling can take six years or more to bear, and around half of all seedlings turn out to be male trees that never fruit at all, so propagated named stock is well worth the extra cost. Marcotted trees come true and fruit young but have no taproot, so stake them firmly against wind. Check the label for the cultivar name, look for a clean graft union and dark glossy leaves, and remember to line up a pollen partner. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties and tree types on sale right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until a rambutan tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted or marcotted named tree usually starts bearing about three to five years after planting. An unnamed seedling can take six years or more, will not come true to its parent, and has roughly a one in two chance of being a male that never fruits, which is why propagated named stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow a rambutan tree in a pot?
faqs.a: While it is young, yes, and a pot lets you nurse a tree through cool weather or stand it in your warmest, most humid corner. But rambutan is a big, thirsty, long-lived tropical tree that crops best in the ground in a genuinely tropical spot, so treat a pot as a way to start a tree or keep one alive in a marginal climate, not as a permanent home for a bearing tree.

faqs.q: How big does a rambutan tree get, and can I keep it smaller?
faqs.a: A seedling can tower to 15 metres or more, but a grafted or marcotted tree is smaller and more spreading, and most growers prune it after harvest to hold it at a pickable 4 to 6 metres. Keeping it low also makes it far easier to throw a net over the canopy, which you will need against birds.

faqs.q: Is a rambutan the same as a lychee?
faqs.a: They are close cousins in the same family and grow much the same way, but a rambutan is the hairy red or yellow one, with sweet-tart flesh that is less perfumed than a lychee. Rambutan is also fussier: it is more tender to cold and dry spells than a lychee or a longan, and where those two will fruit on a single tree, a rambutan usually wants a second tree nearby to pollinate it.

QLD.intro: Growing rambutan in Queensland. The wet tropical coast behind Cairns is the heart of Australia's rambutan growing, one of only two parts of the country (with the Top End of the Northern Territory) reliably warm and humid enough for a crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rambutan wants warmth and humidity all year, high rainfall, and freedom from both frost and dry stress, which on the mainland really means the wet tropical coast of far north Queensland. The deciding factor is how far south you are: the crop is commercially dependable only in the high-rainfall coastal belt, with the southern limit for good production around Innisfail and only mediocre crops as far down as the Sarina district. Below that, trees may grow but fruit poorly.

sections.heading: Where rambutan grows in QLD
sections.body: Almost all of it is in the high-rainfall coastal strip within about 150 kilometres of Cairns: Innisfail, Tully and Mission Beach, Babinda and South Johnstone, and up into the Daintree. The wettest foothill country, such as around Mount Bartle Frere, grows it well. Rambutan has been planted around Cairns for the better part of a century, but it remains a small, specialist industry rather than a mainstream crop.

sections.heading: Which varieties suit QLD
sections.body: The named clones grown commercially in the far north are the same benchmarks sold to home growers: Malaysian R134 and R162, Jitlee, the Indonesian Binjai and the Thai Rongrien among them. Red-skinned clones are generally favoured because they stand up to insects and birds a little better than the yellow types.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's rambutan season runs from about February to June, noticeably later than the Northern Territory crop, which comes in around December to February. Because the Top End fruits first and Queensland follows, Australian rambutan is on the market across a long stretch of the warm season. In the far north the main crop on the wet foothills tends to peak from March into May.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: The single biggest problem is winged raiders: rainbow lorikeets and other parrots, plus flying foxes, can strip a ripening tree, and overhead netting is the standard and often essential defence. Among insects, the fruit-spotting bug blemishes the fruit, and red-banded thrips, mealybugs and scales (with the sooty mould that follows them) infest flushes and flowers. Wet-season fungal troubles include stem canker and fruit and stem-end rots. One welcome point: rambutan is not listed as a host of Queensland fruit fly on the Business Queensland host list or in the Australian fruit fly handbook, so the fly that plagues most Queensland fruit largely leaves it alone.

faqs.q: Will rambutan fruit south of the wet tropics, around Townsville or Mackay?
faqs.a: It gets unreliable fast. Production is dependable only in the high-rainfall coastal belt of the far north, and the crop tails off to mediocre by about the Sarina district, so around Townsville or Mackay you would be relying on a warm, humid, frost-free pocket and watering heavily, and even then crops are light. Further north on the wet coast is the safe country.

faqs.q: Why did my north Queensland rambutan suddenly drop its small green fruit?
faqs.a: Almost always heat and water stress. Rambutan cannot cope with hot, dry, sunny spells while the fruit is filling and reacts by dropping its half-grown fruit, even on a tree that flowered and set heavily. The fix is to keep the humidity up and the water steady, using extra sprinklers or a fine mist through the hottest part of the day rather than letting the tree dry out.

WA.intro: Growing rambutan in Western Australia. Rambutan is an equatorial tree, so in WA it sits right at, and really beyond, the climate margin: the only conceivable ground is the tropical far north, and even there it is unproven, while Perth and the south-west are out of the question.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rambutan needs warmth and humidity the year round, with night temperatures staying above about 15 degrees and no frost ever. WA's tropical far north has the daytime heat but a sharp dry season with cooler nights, and the south-west has a Mediterranean climate of dry summers and cool, wet, sometimes frosty winters that the tree simply cannot take. Growers in central Queensland already have to wrap young trunks against cold below 8 degrees, so nowhere in settled WA offers the steady equatorial warmth a dependable crop needs.

sections.heading: Where it might grow in WA
sections.body: The one realistic candidate is the Ord River Irrigation Area around Kununurra in the East Kimberley, WA's tropical-fruit district, and DPIRD has named rambutan among possible future crops to trial there. In practice the tropical fruit DPIRD actually grows at Kununurra is mango, along with banana and lime, and there is no documented WA-grown rambutan crop. The irrigated Gascoyne around Carnarvon, the warmest mainstream horticulture district, has winters too cool for it.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: Any far-north WA tree that did fruit would follow the Top End pattern rather than the Queensland one, ripening through the wet season from about October into January, earlier than the eastern crop.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: WA's distance and strict plant quarantine keep out several pests of the eastern states, and the fruit fly established here is Mediterranean fruit fly rather than Queensland fruit fly (though rambutan is not a recorded fruit-fly host either way). The unavoidable pests would be the same birds and, in the tropical north, flying foxes that raid the crop everywhere, so any fruiting tree would need netting.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from other states must meet strict import conditions and be permitted entry, so many eastern nurseries will not post here, and rambutan stock is scarce in any case. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Can I grow a rambutan tree in Perth?
faqs.a: No. Perth's dry summers and cool, wet, sometimes frosty winters are a long way outside what this equatorial tree can survive, let alone fruit on. Even a heated, sheltered position will not give you a crop. If you want a tropical-flavoured fruit in Perth, you are far better off with a hardier subtropical chosen for the Mediterranean climate.

faqs.q: Is rambutan grown anywhere in Western Australia?
faqs.a: Not as a documented crop. The tropical Kimberley around Kununurra is the only part of the state warm enough to consider it, and even there it is unproven and has only been floated as a future trial crop. The tropical fruit you can actually grow in far-north WA is mango, with banana and lime.

NSW.intro: Growing rambutan in New South Wales. Rambutan is a strictly tropical tree, and even the warm far north coast of NSW is too cool in winter for a dependable crop, so it is much harder here than its hardier cousins the lychee and longan.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rambutan drops its young leaves, flowers and fruit once nights fall below about 15 degrees, and frost kills it outright. Even within the tropics of the Northern Territory it stops fruiting south of about 13 degrees of latitude, where dry-season nights turn cool. The NSW far north coast lies near 29 degrees south, with winter nights well below that threshold and frost not far inland, so a rambutan here may cling to life in the warmest pocket but rarely sets a worthwhile crop.

sections.heading: Where it might grow in NSW
sections.body: The only ground worth a try is the warmest, most humid, frost-free coastal corners of the far north, the Northern Rivers country around Lismore, Ballina and the Tweed, and even there a rambutan is a backyard curiosity rather than a crop. The cousins lychee and longan take the Northern Rivers winters in their stride; rambutan, which aborts in cool nights, generally does not.

sections.heading: Harvest and protection in NSW
sections.body: In the rare warm pocket where a tree does set fruit, it would ripen over the warmest months of the year. Birds and flying foxes take rambutan wherever it grows, so any fruiting tree, even a single backyard one, needs netting as the fruit colours up.

faqs.q: Can I grow rambutan on the NSW north coast like lychee?
faqs.a: It is far harder. Lychee and longan tolerate the cooler winters of the Northern Rivers and crop there, but rambutan sheds its leaves and fruit once nights drop below about 15 degrees, so it seldom fruits even on the warm coast. If you want a similar fruit that actually crops in northern NSW, plant a lychee or a longan instead.

faqs.q: Would a rambutan survive a frost in northern NSW?
faqs.a: No. Frost kills rambutan, and even cold but frost-free nights make it drop its leaves, flowers and small fruit. Only a genuinely frost-free, sheltered coastal pocket gives a tree any chance of surviving, and fruit there is a bonus, not something to count on.

VIC.intro: Growing rambutan in Victoria. Victoria is far too cold for rambutan: the tree is killed by frost and damaged by any cool spell, so here it is at best a heated-glasshouse curiosity and never a backyard crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rambutan needs warm, humid days of roughly 22 to 32 degrees all year and nights that stay above about 15 degrees, and it dies in frost. Victoria's cold, frosty winters and mild summers are nowhere near this, so an outdoor rambutan will not survive, let alone fruit, anywhere in the state. There is no Victorian district where it can be grown in the open.

sections.heading: Growing one under cover
sections.body: The only way to keep a rambutan alive in Victoria is in a large pot in a warm, bright, frost-free heated glasshouse or conservatory held above about 18 degrees with high humidity, treated as a foliage specimen rather than a fruit tree. It is demanding and rarely rewarding. For a tropical-tasting fruit that actually crops in Victoria, a hardier subtropical such as feijoa or loquat is a far safer bet.

faqs.q: Can I grow a rambutan tree outdoors in Melbourne?
faqs.a: No. Frost and cold winters kill it, and even cool nights make it drop its leaves, so it cannot live outdoors in Melbourne or anywhere in Victoria. The only way to keep one alive is under heated cover, and even then it will not give you fruit.

faqs.q: What tropical-style fruit can I actually grow in Victoria instead?
faqs.a: Skip rambutan and plant a hardier subtropical. A feijoa or a loquat crops happily in much of Victoria, and in the warmest, most sheltered spots a potted tamarillo will fruit too. These give you a taste of the tropics without the heated glasshouse a rambutan would demand.
